title: 2026-06-17 Education and Workforce Committee
displayTitle: 2026-06-17 - Education and Workforce Committee - 2026-06-17 Education and Workforce Committee

chunks.text: All right. Committee, we have our final submitter for the afternoon. We have Nicholas Bernhardt from Informed365. Hopefully, Nicholas, you've accepted the invitation to become a panelist. There you go. Look at that. Efficient. Welcome. Hello. We have 10 minutes. Hello, how are you? Very well. We have 10 minutes with you this afternoon.

That 10 minutes includes time for questions from the committee. You can take your submission as read, but it's always good to go through and highlight the things that you want us to be well across. Perfect. Do you want me to start now? Yes, please. Go for it. Sorry. Perfect. No, kia ora. I'm Nicholas Bernhardt from Informed365, and I appreciate the opportunity to speak to the committee today.

For context, we work with around 500 organizations across all sectors in both Australia and New Zealand, including more than 150 that work through industry consortia on modern slavery compliance and supplier risk management. So we see directly what works and what doesn't in practice, and in this sort of session, I'd love to make three very clear points. The first one is proportionality.

So I've read the submissions to this bill, and there's sort of a real concern that emerges. The idea that every business should do the same level of due diligence, regardless of size or actual risk, that's not really how it works in practice. These sort of small and medium-sized businesses we work with tell us they're overwhelmed.

They're drowning in complex surveys and compliance demands from their larger customers, even when their risk sometimes is very low. And here's the thing, that approach doesn't really help. It's not good due diligence because real due diligence sort of aligned with international standards is about understanding risk and acting proportion to that risk.

So when large entities shift compliance burden down to smaller suppliers just because they can, that's not responsible action. It creates compliance theater, not really protection. So that's point one. The second point that I wanted to raise was around vulnerability. As we all know, modern slavery is fundamentally about the abuse of vulnerability. Exploited workers-- Look, exploiters basically target workers who have no power.

In both our business environments, in both New Zealand and Australia, there are power imbalances every single day. A worker's personal circumstances, business practices, lack of legal protection, all of that matters. So what we're sort of encouraging businesses to do is flip that mindset. Use vulnerability as a framework, which then means three things.

One is a pretty obvious one, take responsibility for your own workers, of course, but also for your indirect workers. And that's people throughout your supply chain that are at risk. Second, listen to workers' voices, which is probably the most valuable data you can find on modern slavery risk.

And work with partners to understand worker views safely and meaningfully. The third one in that vulnerability space is, and this one's crucial, is a preventative mindset. Stop trying to react to problems after they happen, but rather set basic standards and address the systemic factors that create that vulnerability in the first place.

Been doing this now for quite a few years in Australia, and that seems to work really well. The last point that I'd like to make is around impact, not paperwork, which is quite a contentious one sometimes, even across the Tasman, but it is probably the most important point as well.

Reporting requirements are increasing. That's fine. That's perfectly fine. But what our clients tell us is that paper shuffling does not equal impact. You can file reports all day and change nothing for vulnerable workers. What actually works is measuring effectiveness. Clear metrics that allow businesses to, A, identify where their approach isn't working and fix it, direct resources to what actually matters, and communicate honestly to stakeholders. And that sort of is a real demonstration of compliance, not just box ticking, but

actual progress. And so what we're sort of very much in favor of the bill sort of promoting and rewarding is it should incentivize businesses to measure what works, not just measure how they're reporting. And that's, I think, the shift we need to see bearing in mind or taking into account the lessons we've learnt in the UK and in Australia.

That's the three points that I wanted to make. So proportionality, vulnerability, and then impact, not paperwork. Hopefully, that was helpful. Thank you very much, Nicholas. Can I just ask you, coming to us from somewhere tropical, you look like you're a different weather or climate to us in New Zealand.

Look, I'd love to say it's not tropical and beautiful here, but I'm coming from Currumbin Valley in Queensland. Ah. It's very nice. Right. That explains it Most of the things behind me you see, I planted them with these hands, basically. So I feel very special about and very privileged to be in a spot like this.

Thank you, Nicholas, and helpful to know that you've gone through the Australian experience, which has added some insight to your submission. It doesn't appear that we have any questions for you, but can I just say it's not for a lack of interest. You've given us a really constructive and insightful submission in both written and verbally, too. So thank you for being part of the process. It's very helpful to us.
Appreciate it. You take care and let me know if I can help in any other way. Thank you so much. Thanks very much. Bye. Bye.
